restaurants.name: Northcote
restaurants.about: Nestled in the folds of the Ribble Valley at the edge of the Forest of Bowland, Northcote is one of the great institutions of British gastronomy. The restored Victorian manor house has been a beacon of northern fine dining since 1983, and under Chef Patron Lisa Goodwin-Allen, its Michelin-starred kitchen continues to set the standard for bold, seasonal cooking rooted in the extraordinary larder of Lancashire. Lisa draws directly on her landscape — biodynamic farms, the Ribble Valley's celebrated dairy, and her own kitchen garden — to create menus of vivid flavour, precision and deep regional pride.

restaurants.name: The Three Fishes
restaurants.about: Set in a beautifully restored sixteenth-century coaching inn on the banks of the Ribble, The Three Fishes is Nigel Haworth's most personal project — a love letter to the landscape and larder of Lancashire. The legendary Michelin-starred chef, who spent three decades putting the north of England on the fine dining map, has returned to his roots with a farm-to-fork vision of remarkable purity. Behind the whitewashed walls, an organic kitchen garden and edible forest supply much of what appears on the plate, and the menus change with the seasons as food inevitably should.

restaurants.name: sō-lō
restaurants.about: Tucked into a warmly renovated former pub on a quiet lane in the West Lancashire village of Aughton, sō-lō is the long-awaited first solo venture of one of Britain's most decorated chefs. Tim Allen's tasting menus are a study in understated luxury — the informality of the surroundings belies the extraordinary mastery at work in the open kitchen. Seasonal produce from local farmers and the restaurant's own growing beds arrives daily, and every dish is a precise, flavour-driven expression of the land around it. It is exactly the kind of restaurant that makes people travel — and stay.
restaurants.address: 17 Town Green Lane, Aughton, Ormskirk L39 6SE

restaurants.name: The Kitchin
restaurants.about: Housed in a converted whisky bonded warehouse on the historic waterfront at Leith, The Kitchin is one of the most celebrated restaurants in Scotland. Tom Kitchin's 'From Nature to Plate' philosophy — forged in the kitchens of Pierre Koffmann, Alain Ducasse and Guy Savoy — finds its fullest expression here, in dishes of astonishing depth that honour Scotland's extraordinary natural larder. Langoustines arrive live, game comes from the hills in season, and the sea provides whatever is at its absolute best. A glass-walled kitchen lets diners share in the theatre.

restaurants.name: The Scran & Scallie
restaurants.about: On a leafy corner of Edinburgh's Stockbridge neighbourhood, The Scran & Scallie is the gastropub that Tom and Michaela Kitchin built for their community — and for themselves. The interior, with its sheepskin throws, original brickwork and hint of Scandinavian warmth, is as inviting as any room in the city. The food follows Tom's 'From Nature to Plate' ethos faithfully: butchering and filleting done in-house, ingredients sourced from the finest Scottish suppliers, and a menu of proper seasonal pub cooking that spans steak pie, Cullen Skink and shellfish ravioli with equal conviction.
restaurants.address: 1 Comely Bank Road, Stockbridge, Edinburgh EH4 1DR

restaurants.name: Restaurant Martin Wishart
restaurants.about: On the historic shore of Leith, Martin Wishart's eponymous restaurant holds a unique place in Scottish culinary history — Edinburgh's first Michelin star, held with quiet authority for over two decades. The bright, elegant dining room overlooking the Water of Leith is the setting for cooking that is deceptively simple in presentation yet extraordinary in depth: top Scottish shellfish, Orkney scallops, Borders game and the finest seasonal produce treated with the precision of classical French technique and the instinctive sensitivity of a chef who has spent thirty years in dedicated pursuit of the perfect plate.

restaurants.name: Chez Bruce
restaurants.about: Overlooking Wandsworth Common in south-west London, Chez Bruce has been voted Londoners' favourite restaurant more times than almost any other. Bruce Poole opened it in 1995 on the site of Marco Pierre White's legendary Harveys — but where his predecessor was all theatre, Poole is all substance. The cooking is modern British with a deeply felt French and Mediterranean soul: gutsy braises, home-made charcuterie, an extraordinary cheese board and a wine list that is widely regarded as one of the finest in the capital. There is nothing fussy here, nothing redundant — just beautifully cooked food and the warm conviction that a restaurant's job is to make people happy.
restaurants.address: 2 Bellevue Road, Wandsworth Common, London SW17 7EG

restaurants.name: La Trompette
restaurants.about: Tucked away on a side street off Chiswick High Road, La Trompette has been the jewel of west London's dining scene since it opened in 2001. The sister restaurant to Chez Bruce, it shares the same conviction that great food need not be formal, and that the finest ingredients — largely British, with a confident French accent — speak best when treated with restraint and respect. The dining room is warm and unhurried, the wine list genuinely thrilling, and the cooking by head chef Rob Weston is as assured as any in the capital: precise, generous, and built on flavours that are always absolutely true.

restaurants.name: Pied à Terre
restaurants.about: On Charlotte Street in the heart of Fitzrovia, Pied à Terre holds a distinction earned by very few: London's longest-standing Michelin-starred restaurant, a beacon of independent fine dining since 1991. Founded by David Moore and guided by a succession of brilliant head chefs — from Richard Neat and Tom Aikens to Shane Osborn and now Alberto Cavaliere — it is a restaurant defined by restless ambition and immaculate hospitality. The intimate, copper-and-leather dining room is the setting for cooking that balances classical French rigour with contemporary creativity, underpinned by a legendary wine cellar that has few equals in the capital.

restaurants.name: Galvin La Chapelle
restaurants.about: Housed in a magnificent Grade II listed former Victorian school chapel moments from Spitalfields Market, Galvin La Chapelle is one of the most dramatically beautiful dining rooms in London. The soaring vaulted ceiling, arched windows and warm amber lighting create an atmosphere of effortless glamour, and the cooking by brothers Chris and Jeff Galvin is equal to the setting. Their French cuisine — classical in technique, light in touch, emphatically seasonal — has earned this beloved family restaurant a Michelin star and a permanent place in the hearts of London food lovers who return again and again for the bold, harmonious flavours and the exceptional wine list.

restaurants.name: The Angel at Hetton
restaurants.about: Widely regarded as Britain's original gastropub, The Angel at Hetton has stood at the heart of the Yorkshire Dales since the fifteenth century. In 2018, chef patron Michael Wignall — whose extraordinary career has produced Michelin stars at Pennyhill Park, Gidleigh Park and beyond — arrived with his wife Johanna and transformed this ancient inn into one of the most exciting restaurants in England. His cooking is modern, technical and deeply meaningful: every element on the plate earns its place, every flavour is vivid and precise, and the whole is anchored in the dramatic countryside that surrounds it.
restaurants.address: Hetton, near Skipton, North Yorkshire BD23 6LT

restaurants.name: The Star Inn at Harome
restaurants.about: Beneath the thatch of a fourteenth-century inn on the edge of the North York Moors, Andrew Pern has spent thirty years defining what modern Yorkshire cooking can be. The Star is the place that helped invent the gastropub — earning its Michelin star in 2002 as only the second pub in the world to do so — and it has never lost its soul. Pern's 'Modern Yorkshire' menus are rooted in the extraordinary pantry of God's Own County: North Sea fish, Moorland game, heritage vegetables and the hand-made cheeses and charcuterie of the farms that surround it. Honest, generous and deeply rooted in place.

restaurants.name: Pipe and Glass
restaurants.about: On the edge of the Dalton Park Estate in the rolling Yorkshire Wolds, the Pipe and Glass is James and Kate Mackenzie's life's work — a seventeenth-century former coaching inn transformed into East Yorkshire's only Michelin-starred restaurant. James's cooking is a warm, generously proportioned celebration of the region's finest produce: game from the surrounding estates, fish from the North Sea coast, and vegetables from the pub's own kitchen garden and herbarium. The dining room is all wood-burners, tartan tweed and easy conviviality — a place where locals and destination diners sit side by side, united by the pleasure of exceptional food.
restaurants.address: West End, South Dalton, Beverley HU17 7PN

restaurants.about: At Trinity Gardens in the heart of Newcastle, 21 is the flagship of Terry Laybourne's remarkable 21 Hospitality Group — and the restaurant that helped put the North East on the fine dining map. Opened in 1988 as 21 Queen Street, it became the region's first Michelin-starred restaurant within three years, and Laybourne's tenure earned him an MBE and the Freedom of the City. Today, 21 is a sleek, light-filled brasserie de luxe: generous, ingredient-led and free of unnecessary artifice, with the emphasis firmly on good produce treated with skill, warm hospitality and the conviction that great food belongs in every great city.
restaurants.address: Trinity Gardens, Newcastle upon Tyne NE1 2HH

restaurants.name: The Art School
restaurants.about: Inside a stunning Victorian building on Sugnall Street, a stone's throw from the Philharmonic Hall, The Art School is chef patron Paul Askew's decade-long love letter to Liverpool. Since opening in 2014, it has been the city's most ambitious and consistent fine dining destination — a restaurant of genuine elegance and conviction, where seasonal produce from the North West's finest artisan suppliers is handled with skill and deep respect. Paul's cooking, shaped by years in Singapore, Dubai and New York as well as the very best of British kitchens, is bold, colourful and alive with the flavours of a chef who has spent a career shaping what Liverpool can be on the national and international gastronomic stage.

restaurants.name: Opheem
restaurants.about: In Birmingham's Jewellery Quarter, Opheem stands as one of the most significant restaurants to open in Britain this century. Aktar Islam, born and raised in the city to Bangladeshi parents, has created something genuinely new: a cooking language that draws on the depth and complexity of the Indian subcontinent — its Mughal banquets, ancient manuscripts and regional traditions — while remaining absolutely rooted in the finest seasonal British produce. The result, honoured with two Michelin stars in 2024, is food of extraordinary intensity, precision and cultural meaning. A restaurant that has changed the conversation about what Indian fine dining can be.

restaurants.name: El Gato Negro Tapas
restaurants.about: Spread across three floors of a handsome period townhouse on King Street in the heart of Manchester, El Gato Negro is one of the most exhilarating dining rooms in the north of England. Chef patron Simon Shaw — who held a Michelin Bib Gourmand at his original Ripponden site for years before relocating to the city in 2016 — has created a joyful, modern expression of Spanish tapas culture: small plates of vivid flavour and bold technique, informed by the seasons and the finest local and Iberian produce, best enjoyed in the company of exceptional Spanish wine. The rooftop terrace, open beneath the Manchester sky, is something approaching magical.

restaurants.name: Mark Poynton at Caistor Hall
restaurants.about: Set within ninety acres of sweeping Norfolk countryside in a beautifully restored Georgian country house, Mark Poynton at Caistor Hall is the finest table in East Anglia. Mark Poynton — whose career has taken him from the legendary kitchens of Paul Kitching and Daniel Clifford to his own Michelin-starred ventures in Cambridge — arrived here in 2023 with an ambition to open the best restaurant in Norfolk. The Michelin Guide awarded him a star in 2025. His tasting menus are a masterclass in light, technically precise, season-driven cooking: local day-boat fish, East Anglian artisan produce and the very best ingredients from further afield, treated with unfailing intelligence and care.
restaurants.address: Stoke Road, Caistor St Edmund, Norwich NR14 8QN

restaurants.name: Ancient Shepherds
restaurants.about: In the quiet riverside village of Fen Ditton on the eastern edge of Cambridge, Ancient Shepherds is Mark Poynton's neighbourhood restaurant — intimate, unhurried and deeply personal. The former pub, lovingly restored, is the setting for cooking of remarkable warmth and intelligence: seasonal menus built around the finest Cambridgeshire and East Anglian produce, handled with the technical assurance of a chef who has held Michelin stars and the emotional directness of one who simply loves to cook. A short drive from the city's famous colleges, it is the kind of place that regulars guard as their own quiet secret.
restaurants.address: High Street, Fen Ditton, Cambridge CB5 8ST

chef-lisa.name: Lisa Goodwin-Allen
chef-lisa.title: Chef Patron & Director
chef-lisa.bio:
Lisa Goodwin-Allen joined the Northcote brigade in 2001 as a demi-chef de partie and, at just twenty-three, became head chef — one of the youngest in the country to hold such a position at a restaurant of this standing. She has led the kitchen ever since, earning and retaining the Michelin star that Northcote has held since 1996, and becoming one of the most admired and celebrated chefs in Britain.

Her cooking is a vivid expression of the Ribble Valley landscape — bold, technically assured, and rooted in relationships with local farmers, biodynamic growers and the kitchen garden she tends with genuine devotion. A finalist for the Roux Scholarship, winner of the Great British Menu, and a familiar face on British television, Lisa is now Chef Patron and Director, overseeing a new era of ambition at Northcote under its recently returned independent ownership.

chef-nigel.name: Nigel Haworth
chef-nigel.title: Chef Patron
chef-nigel.accolades: Former Michelin Star · Egon Ronay Chef of the Year
chef-nigel.bio:
Nigel Haworth is one of the defining figures of British gastronomy north of the Watford Gap. Born in Lancashire, he trained at Gleneagles and the Ritz before returning to his home county to take on the head chef position at Northcote in the mid-1980s, where he earned a Michelin star in the mid-1990s and held it for two decades — putting the Ribble Valley on the international fine dining map.

In 2021, having stepped back from Northcote to an ambassadorial role, he acquired The Three Fishes in Mitton with a new ambition: to build a farm-to-fork project of extraordinary integrity, rooted in zero waste, organic growing and honest, seasonal British cooking. His one-acre kitchen garden and edible forest are as much a statement of values as they are a source of produce.

chef-tim-allen.name: Tim Allen
chef-tim-allen.title: Chef Owner
chef-tim-allen.bio:
Born in Huddersfield, Tim Allen has spent thirty years earning Michelin stars at some of England's finest restaurants — at Whatley Manor alongside Martin Burge, at the Wild Rabbit in Kingham, at Launceston Place in London, and at the Flitch of Bacon in Essex. He has been awarded stars at four separate establishments and competed on the BBC's Great British Menu.

In 2021, Tim opened sō-lō in the Lancashire village of Aughton — his first truly solo venture, and the culmination of everything he has learned. The restaurant was awarded its Michelin star in 2023, just seventeen months after opening. His cooking is contemporary, seasonally precise and deceptively relaxed, with each dish designed to let exceptional produce — much of it grown on the premises or sourced from trusted local farms — speak with absolute clarity.

chef-tom-kitchin.name: Tom Kitchin
chef-tom-kitchin.title: Chef Owner & Founder
chef-tom-kitchin.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · 5 AA Rosettes · Scottish Chef of the Year
chef-tom-kitchin.bio:
Edinburgh-born Tom Kitchin trained in some of the world's greatest kitchens — under Pierre Koffmann at La Tante Claire, with Alain Ducasse at Le Louis XV in Monaco, and at Guy Savoy in Paris — before returning to Scotland to open The Kitchin in 2006. Six months later, at just twenty-nine, he was awarded a Michelin star, making him Scotland's youngest Michelin-starred chef proprietor.

His 'From Nature to Plate' philosophy has since become a guiding principle of Scottish fine dining: ingredients sourced directly from land and sea, arriving fresh daily, treated with reverence and cooked with the technical brilliance of a chef shaped by the very finest classical traditions. Tom's restaurants now include The Kitchin, The Scran & Scallie and The Bonnie Badger, and he remains a constant presence on the pass at Leith.

chef-martin-wishart.name: Martin Wishart
chef-martin-wishart.title: Chef Patron
chef-martin-wishart.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · 24 Consecutive Years · Honorary Degree, University of Edinburgh
chef-martin-wishart.bio:
Martin Wishart was raised in the Blackhall area of Edinburgh and left school at fifteen with no particular plan — until a job as a kitchen apprentice changed the course of his life entirely. He worked across Scotland, then left for France to knock on the door of three-starred Marc Meneau in Burgundy. A formative career followed, with stints under Albert Roux, Marco Pierre White, Charlie Trotter and Michel Roux, before Martin returned to Edinburgh with a singular ambition.

In 1999, he opened Restaurant Martin Wishart on the shore at Leith — tiling the kitchen and painting the walls himself. In 2001, it became Edinburgh's first Michelin-starred restaurant. Martin has held that star for every year since, making him one of Scotland's most enduring and respected figures in gastronomy, and a mentor to a generation of Edinburgh chefs who have gone on to define the city's remarkable dining scene.

chef-bruce-poole.name: Bruce Poole
chef-bruce-poole.title: Chef Patron & Co-Owner
chef-bruce-poole.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · Voted London's Favourite Restaurant 11 Years Running
chef-bruce-poole.bio:
Bruce Poole studied history at Exeter University before discovering cooking — a trajectory that suits both the intellectual rigour and the deep pleasure his food consistently delivers. After training at Westminster Hotel and Catering College, he worked under Simon Hopkinson at Bibendum and Phil Howard at The Square before being asked by restaurateur Nigel Platts-Martin to take on the Wandsworth site vacated by Marco Pierre White.

In 1995, Chez Bruce opened. In 1999, it was awarded its first Michelin star. For more than a decade it topped the Harden's poll of Londoners' favourite restaurant, an accolade that speaks to something beyond critical acclaim — a restaurant that people genuinely love. Together with Platts-Martin, Bruce went on to open La Trompette in Chiswick and The Glasshouse in Kew. His cooking remains defined by honest flavour, instinctive craft and a complete absence of pretension.

chef-rob-weston.name: Rob Weston
chef-rob-weston.title: Head Chef
chef-rob-weston.bio:
Rob Weston is a chef of rare steadiness and depth — a cook who has spent his career in the shadow of some of Britain's finest kitchens and emerged with a voice entirely his own. After formative years working with Marco Pierre White, a stint at Guy Savoy in Paris, and training at Le Gavroche under Michel Roux Jr, he became head chef at The Square alongside Phil Howard, where he remained for fifteen years.

In 2013 he joined La Trompette in Chiswick, and under his tenure the restaurant has continued to earn its place as one of the most consistently brilliant in London. His cooking is generous and precise in equal measure: British ingredients — Cornish seafood, Devonshire duck, the very best seasonal produce — elevated by the classical technique and Mediterranean sensibility that define the La Trompette kitchen.

chef-alberto-cavaliere.name: Alberto Cavaliere
chef-alberto-cavaliere.title: Head Chef
chef-alberto-cavaliere.bio:
British-born Alberto Cavaliere arrived at Pied à Terre in January 2025, bringing with him a career shaped by some of the most demanding and creative kitchens in the world — Marcus Belgravia, Joël Robuchon's London operation, and the celebrated Basque-inspired Sabor. His cooking synthesises the rigorous classical French tradition of the restaurant with his own instinctive curiosity, drawing on Asian influences, exquisite seasonal British produce and a technical assurance that has impressed both critics and a loyal clientele.

At a restaurant that has been London's longest-standing Michelin-starred establishment since 1991, Alberto carries a considerable inheritance with evident confidence, bringing a new chapter of invention to a kitchen that has shaped many of Britain's finest chefs.

chef-jeff-galvin.name: Jeff Galvin
chef-jeff-galvin.title: Chef Patron & Co-Founder
chef-jeff-galvin.bio:
Jeff Galvin and his brother Chris are among the most admired figures in British French cooking — Essex-born chefs whose combined career spans more than six decades and includes formative years at The Savoy, The Ritz, L'Escargot, The Lanesborough and The Wolseley. Together, they have built one of London's most beloved restaurant groups, guided always by the same conviction: that classical French cuisine, executed with skill and served without formality, is one of life's great pleasures.

At Galvin La Chapelle, Jeff leads the kitchen in a setting of jaw-dropping drama — the soaring vaulted ceiling of a Victorian school chapel framing food that is equally ambitious. The Michelin star arrived in 2011, just a year after opening, and has been held with quiet consistency ever since. Brother David sources the produce each morning, bringing the family ethos full circle from market to plate.

chef-michael-wignall.name: Michael Wignall
chef-michael-wignall.title: Chef Patron
chef-michael-wignall.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · 5 AA Rosettes · National Restaurant Awards Chef of the Year 2022
chef-michael-wignall.bio:
Michael Wignall has won or retained a Michelin star at every kitchen he has led — a distinction held by almost no one else in Britain. Born in Preston and raised in the Lake District, he began his professional career under Paul Heathcote before ascending through Waldo's at Cliveden, The Devonshire Arms and Michael's Nook. At Pennyhill Park's Latymer restaurant he built a kitchen of national significance, earning two Michelin stars and five AA Rosettes. He then retained two stars at the legendary Gidleigh Park in Devon.

In 2018, he and his wife Johanna took on The Angel at Hetton — Britain's original gastropub — and transformed it quietly but completely. The Michelin star arrived within twelve months. His food is complex and technically exacting, yet every dish is animated by warmth, by the deep flavours of Yorkshire's landscape, and by the simple conviction that food should move people.

chef-andrew-pern.name: Andrew Pern
chef-andrew-pern.title: Chef Patron
chef-andrew-pern.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · Michelin Star held 20+ years total
chef-andrew-pern.bio:
Andrew Pern grew up near Whitby on the edge of the North York Moors — and has spent thirty years cooking its landscape back to the table. He became head chef at Rosedale Abbey at twenty-one, then in 1996 acquired The Star Inn at Harome, a fourteenth-century thatched inn that became one of Britain's first gastropubs and, in 2002, only the second pub in the world to earn a Michelin star.

Andrew's 'Modern Yorkshire' cooking is an act of deep regional allegiance: game from the Moors, shellfish from the North Sea coast, hare and venison from the surrounding estates, heritage vegetables from the kitchen garden. His signature foie gras and black pudding — rich man's ingredient, poor man's ingredient, united on one plate — is one of the most celebrated dishes in the north of England. In 2026, he marks thirty years of ownership at The Star.

chef-james-mackenzie.name: James Mackenzie
chef-james-mackenzie.title: Chef Owner
chef-james-mackenzie.bio:
James Mackenzie grew up in Filey on the North Yorkshire coast and was washing dishes in a restaurant by the age of thirteen. He trained at Scarborough Technical College — alongside Andrew Pern and James Martin — before working his way through prestigious kitchens and eventually becoming head chef at Andrew Pern's Star Inn at Harome. It was there he found his calling: unpretentious British cooking, deeply rooted in the landscape, built on bold flavours and honest technique.

In 2006, James and his wife Kate took on the Pipe and Glass in South Dalton — a seventeenth-century coaching inn on the edge of the Dalton Estate — and transformed it into East Yorkshire's only Michelin-starred restaurant. The star arrived in 2010 and has been held every year since. James's menus are anchored in the seasons and the Yorkshire Wolds: game from the surrounding fields, fish from the east coast, and vegetables and flowers from the pub's own kitchen garden and herbarium.

chef-terry-laybourne.name: Terry Laybourne
chef-terry-laybourne.title: Founder & Chef Restaurateur
chef-terry-laybourne.accolades: Former Michelin Star · MBE · Freedom of the City of Newcastle
chef-terry-laybourne.bio:
Terry Laybourne is the man who put Newcastle on the fine dining map. Born and raised in the city, he trained in Switzerland and Germany before returning to the north-east with an ambition: to prove that great food did not require a London postcode. In 1988, he opened 21 Queen Street on Newcastle's Quayside with his wife Susan and brother Laurance, in a part of the city the cultural renaissance had not yet reached.

Within three years, the restaurant held the region's first Michelin star. In the years that followed, Terry built the 21 Hospitality Group — a family of venues united by generosity, genuine craft and a deep connection to Tyneside — and was awarded an MBE for services to hospitality. He received the Freedom of the City of Newcastle in recognition of a thirty-five-year contribution to its culinary identity. He remains the most formative single figure in the north-east's restaurant scene.

chef-paul-askew.name: Paul Askew
chef-paul-askew.title: Chef Patron & Owner
chef-paul-askew.accolades: Michelin Recommended · 2 AA Rosettes · Taste of England Award 2017
chef-paul-askew.bio:
Paul Askew grew up on the Wirral before his family moved to Dubai and then Singapore — an early immersion in the flavours of the world that shaped a chef of genuinely cosmopolitan curiosity. He began his career as a kitchen porter at Thornton Hall, trained with De Vere Hotels, and spent formative years at the Philharmonic Hall in Liverpool before joining the London Carriage Works and Hope Street Hotel.

In 2014, he opened The Art School in a stunning Victorian building on Sugnall Street, with an ambition to give Liverpool the fine dining destination it deserved. A decade later, the restaurant is widely regarded as one of the finest in the North of England — Michelin recommended, critically acclaimed, and the foremost expression of Paul's unwavering commitment to the city he loves. He is a fellow of the Royal Academy of Culinary Arts and Chairman of the North of England, and has cooked for G7 delegations, the Turner Prize and the Grand National.

chef-aktar-islam.name: Aktar Islam
chef-aktar-islam.title: Chef Owner
chef-aktar-islam.accolades: 2 Michelin Stars · 4 AA Rosettes · Great British Menu Champion 2011
chef-aktar-islam.bio:
Aktar Islam was born and raised in Birmingham to Bangladeshi parents, and began working in his father's restaurant at the age of thirteen. He is entirely self-taught — and entirely extraordinary. Over a career spanning nearly three decades, he has built one of the most significant bodies of work in British Indian cooking, culminating in Opheem, his flagship restaurant in the city's Jewellery Quarter.

Opened in 2018, Opheem earned its first Michelin star within a year — making it the first Indian restaurant outside London to do so. In 2024, it became Birmingham's first ever two-starred restaurant. Aktar's cooking draws on the cuisine of the royal courts of Mughal India, on the subcontinent's millennia of culinary history, and on the finest seasonal British produce available. It is food of enormous intensity, precision and cultural significance — and a profound statement about what this country's restaurant culture can become.

chef-simon-shaw.name: Simon Shaw
chef-simon-shaw.title: Chef Patron & Creative Director
chef-simon-shaw.accolades: Michelin Bib Gourmand · Good Food Guide Listed
chef-simon-shaw.bio:
Simon Shaw is one of the North of England's most quietly influential figures in the restaurant world — a chef whose forty-year career has been shaped by an abiding passion for the food and wine of Spain. Originally from Yorkshire, he worked his way through the county's finest establishments before becoming the first head chef at Harvey Nichols in Leeds, a role that took him to the flagship Knightsbridge store as Executive Head Chef.

In 2005, he opened El Gato Negro in Ripponden — a Spanish tapas restaurant in a Yorkshire village that quickly became one of the most talked-about restaurants in the north. A Michelin Bib Gourmand followed. In 2016, he relocated to King Street in Manchester, where El Gato Negro has flourished across three floors and a rooftop terrace, earning awards, critical acclaim and the enduring devotion of guests who return again and again for the bold, joyful, seasonal small plates and exceptional Spanish wine.

chef-mark-poynton.name: Mark Poynton
chef-mark-poynton.title: Chef Director
chef-mark-poynton.accolades: 1 Michelin Star · Norfolk Food and Drink Chef of the Year 2024
chef-mark-poynton.bio:
Mark Poynton's career is a study in precision and perseverance. He began in country house hotel kitchens before a transformative period under Paul Kitching at the legendary Juniper in Altrincham — one of the most technically demanding and creatively extraordinary restaurants of its era. From there he joined Daniel Clifford at the two-starred Midsummer House in Cambridge, an apprenticeship in the very highest standards of classical modern cooking.

He earned his own Michelin star at Alimentum in Cambridge, retaining it for five years, before opening further ventures in the city and ultimately arriving at Caistor Hall in Norfolk in 2023. The Michelin star for his restaurant there came in 2025, fulfilling his stated ambition to open the finest table in East Anglia. Mark's cooking is light, technically immaculate and driven by the finest local produce — in particular, the extraordinary line-caught fish and artisan dairy of the East Anglian coast and countryside.

exp-northcote-chefs-table.name: The Chef's Table
exp-northcote-chefs-table.description: Northcote's legendary glass-walled Chef's Table places a small group of guests at the very heart of Lisa Goodwin-Allen's brigade, with front-row views of every stage in the creation of the evening's tasting menu. The experience begins with a private tour of the kitchen garden with Lisa herself, before moving to the Chef's Table for a bespoke tasting menu developed around what is at its absolute best that week, paired with wines from Northcote's award-winning cellar overseen by Managing Director Craig Bancroft.
includes: Private kitchen garden tour with Lisa Goodwin-Allen

exp-three-fishes-garden.name: The Kitchen Garden Morning
exp-three-fishes-garden.tagline: From the earth to the table
exp-three-fishes-garden.description: Begin with a private guided walk of The Three Fishes' one-acre organic kitchen garden and edible forest with Nigel Haworth, discovering the heritage seeds, compost-no-dig beds and polytunnel that supply the kitchen with herbs, vegetables and fruits through every season. Return to the restaurant for a five-course Farm-to-Fork tasting menu — each course built around what was harvested that morning — with wine pairings carefully chosen to complement the clean, honest flavours of the land.
includes: Guided kitchen garden tour with Nigel Haworth

exp-solo-chefs-table.name: The Chef's Table at sō-lō
exp-solo-chefs-table.description: sō-lō's newly installed Chef's Table places guests at the very edge of Tim Allen's open kitchen, immersed in the theatre of a Michelin-starred service. Tim cooks a bespoke extended tasting menu — unpublished, tailored entirely to the evening — with each course presented and explained by Tim himself. The experience includes a pre-dinner tour of the growing beds in the car park and a signed copy of the evening's menu with Tim's handwritten notes on every dish.
includes: Tour of the kitchen garden beds
Handwritten signed menu by Tim Allen

exp-kitchin-prestige.name: The Prestige Experience
exp-kitchin-prestige.tagline: Nature to plate, from beginning to end
exp-kitchin-prestige.description: The Kitchin's celebrated Prestige surprise menu is the fullest expression of Tom Kitchin's 'From Nature to Plate' philosophy — a course-by-course journey through the very finest ingredients Scotland's land and sea can offer on that particular day. Guests are seated by the glass-walled kitchen, watching the brigade at work, and are joined at the table during the meal by Tom himself. The experience includes a private introduction to the morning's market delivery and a guided wine pairing through some of the most exceptional bottles in The Kitchin's cellar.
includes: Market produce introduction with Tom Kitchin

exp-scran-sunday-table.name: The Sunday Roast Table
exp-scran-sunday-table.description: The Scran & Scallie's famous Sunday lunch is one of Edinburgh's most sought-after tables — a generous, warmly convivial feast of Scotland's finest seasonal produce, built around whole roasts brought to the table and carved by the chef. This private Sunday table experience adds a welcome drink in the bar, a tour of the pub's sourcing philosophy with head chef Jamie Knox, and access to the full Sunday menu including a dedicated cheese course and dessert selection, with Scottish ales and carefully chosen wines to match.
includes: Sourcing tour with head chef Jamie Knox
Full Sunday lunch menu with whole-roast carving

exp-wishart-seafood-journey.name: The Seafood Journey
exp-wishart-seafood-journey.tagline: Scotland's finest waters, plate by plate
exp-wishart-seafood-journey.description: Martin Wishart's love of Scottish shellfish is legendary — the langoustines, scallops and crabs arriving live into his kitchen are treated as the precious, wild things they are. This exclusive evening begins with a private tasting of live shellfish with Martin at the kitchen pass before moving to the dining room for a bespoke all-seafood tasting menu, moving through the waters of Scotland from Loch Ryan oysters to Orkney scallops to Hebridean lobster, with a wine pairing chosen from the restaurant's colossal French-led cellar.
includes: Live shellfish tasting at the kitchen pass

exp-chez-bruce-wine.name: The Wine & Cheese Evening
exp-chez-bruce-wine.description: Chez Bruce takes its cheese board and wine list more seriously than almost any restaurant in Britain — and this private evening is a celebration of both. Beginning with a guided tour of the cheese selection with the head sommelier, guests settle into the first-floor private dining room for a six-course menu designed around wine, with each course built specifically to complement a bottle chosen by the sommelier from what is widely considered one of the finest wine lists in the UK. The meal concludes with a dedicated cheese course of eight selections.
includes: Private cheese room tour with head sommelier
Six exceptional wines from the list

exp-trompette-wine-dinner.name: The Sommelier's Table
exp-trompette-wine-dinner.description: La Trompette's wine list is one of the great collections in London — a labour of genuine passion, built over two decades with an independence of spirit that sets it apart from any corporate cellar. This private evening invites a small group to join the restaurant's head sommelier for a journey through its exceptional depths: a six-course dinner in the private dining room, with each course paired to a bottle from a carefully designed flight that spans France, the wider Mediterranean and beyond.
includes: Private tour of the wine programme with head sommelier
Six-course dinner in the private dining room
Tasting notes and take-home wine card

exp-pied-a-terre-cellar.name: The Cellar & Tasting Menu
exp-pied-a-terre-cellar.description: Pied à Terre's wine cellar has been described as one of the finest in London — a collection assembled over thirty years by founder David Moore, who remains personally and passionately involved in every selection. This private evening begins with David himself leading a candlelit tour of the cellar, sharing the stories behind exceptional bottles, before guests move to the intimate dining room for Alberto Cavaliere's full tasting menu, paired to perfection by one of the capital's most celebrated sommelier teams.
includes: Private cellar tour with David Moore
Full tasting menu by Alberto Cavaliere
Paired wine flight from the private cellar

exp-chapelle-vertical.name: The Hermitage Vertical
exp-chapelle-vertical.description: Galvin La Chapelle takes its name from the celebrated Hermitage La Chapelle wine of Paul Jaboulet Aîné — and holds one of the only vertical lists of this legendary Rhône in London. This exclusive private dining evening in the restaurant's mezzanine dining room begins with a guided introduction to the vertical by head sommelier Stephen Nisbet, followed by a six-course menu from Executive Chef Arturo Granato designed to match the wines, moving from delicate whites through to the profound, age-worthy reds of this great appellation.
includes: Vertical Hermitage La Chapelle tasting introduction

exp-angel-dales-table.name: The Dales Table
exp-angel-dales-table.tagline: The moors, the valley, the inn
exp-angel-dales-table.description: The Angel at Hetton sits at the heart of one of the most dramatically beautiful landscapes in England. This private experience begins with a walk through the Dales with Michael Wignall, visiting the local farms and producers who supply his kitchen before returning to the inn for an extended tasting menu that charts the journey from landscape to plate. Each course is accompanied by a wine personally selected by Michael, and the evening concludes beside the fire in the bar with a dram from the inn's remarkable whisky collection.
includes: Producer walk in the Dales with Michael Wignall
Extended tasting menu with table visit

exp-star-inn-game.name: The Game Season Table
exp-star-inn-game.description: From September to February, The Star Inn at Harome becomes the ultimate expression of Yorkshire's extraordinary game larder. Andrew Pern has been cooking grouse, hare, partridge, mallard and venison from the surrounding Moors for thirty years, and this private seasonal tasting menu is the definitive showcase — six courses of game in all its forms, from Andrew's legendary foie gras and black pudding to whole roasted grouse, hare Rossini with yellow chanterelle and truffle, and a finale of Yorkshire cheeses. Available during game season only.
includes: Paired wine flight from the cellar
Signed copy of his cookbook 'Loose Birds & Game'

exp-pipe-glass-private.name: The Hotham Room
exp-pipe-glass-private.description: On the first floor of the Pipe and Glass, overlooking the rolling fields of the Dalton Estate, the private Hotham Room is the setting for James Mackenzie's most personal dining experience. Guests begin in the Chef's Library with champagne and canapés, before moving to the room for a bespoke seasonal tasting menu created specifically for the occasion. Kate Mackenzie's exceptional wine list — one of the most innovative in Yorkshire, organised by ingredient rather than region — is matched to each course by James himself.
includes: Champagne and canapés in the Chef's Library
Bespoke tasting menu by James Mackenzie
Paired wine flight from Kate's list

exp-21-private-dining.name: The Private Dining Room
exp-21-private-dining.description: 21's private dining room is a restaurant within a restaurant — spacious, comfortable and discreetly separated from the main floor, with its own adjoining bar. For a private celebration, business dinner or intimate gathering, the room seats up to twenty guests for an exclusive menu designed in consultation with the kitchen team: bistro dishes of skill and warmth, built on seasonal British produce and served with the genuine northern hospitality that has defined 21 since Terry Laybourne opened the first restaurant in 1988.
includes: Exclusive use of the private dining room
Pre-dinner drinks in the adjoining bar
Paired wine suggestions from the sommelier

exp-art-school-lantern.name: The Lantern Room Experience
exp-art-school-lantern.description: The Art School's spectacular Lantern Room — the centrepiece of the Victorian building on Sugnall Street — is at its most beautiful in the evening light, and this private tasting menu experience is designed to take full advantage of it. Paul Askew creates a bespoke menu for the occasion, drawing on his global influences and the finest produce of the Liverpool City Region, paired with bottles from one of the most ambitious wine cellars in the north of England. Paul joins the table mid-service to share the stories behind the dishes.
includes: Welcome cocktail in The Art School Cellars Bar
Bespoke tasting menu by Paul Askew
Table visit and personal menu talk

exp-opheem-coal-kitchen.name: The Coal Kitchen Table
exp-opheem-coal-kitchen.description: Much of Opheem's cooking passes over, under or through fire — and this private experience places guests at a table beside the coal kitchen itself, watching Aktar Islam's brigade work with the live coals that are central to his cuisine. The evening unfolds as an extended journey through Aktar's tasting menu, each course preceded by a brief account of its culinary heritage — from the Mughal banquets of the Indian royal courts to the seasonal British produce on the plate before you. An exceptional pairing of Indian-influenced cocktails and wines frames the meal.
includes: Extended tasting menu with culinary history commentary

exp-gato-negro-rooftop.name: The Rooftop Table
exp-gato-negro-rooftop.description: El Gato Negro's rooftop terrace — retractable roof open to the Manchester sky in summer, warmly enclosed through winter — is the most exhilarating dining setting in the city. This private rooftop table experience invites a small group to enjoy an extended tour through Simon Shaw's seasonal tapas menu, guided by a member of the kitchen team and paired with a curated selection of exceptional Spanish wines and sherries chosen by Miles Corish, the Master of Wine behind El Gato Negro's celebrated list.
includes: Guided Spanish wine and sherry pairing

exp-caistor-estate-table.name: The Estate Table
exp-caistor-estate-table.tagline: The finest table in East Anglia
exp-caistor-estate-table.description: Set within the Georgian splendour of Caistor Hall, this private dinner for a small group begins with a walk through the hotel's ninety acres with Mark Poynton, taking in the kitchen garden, the walled grounds and the Norfolk countryside that inspires his menus. Guests then settle in the private dining space for an extended version of Mark's tasting menu — a cook who has spent his career learning from the very best, now expressing the extraordinary larder of East Anglia with a lightness of touch and a technical precision that earned his Michelin star in 2025.
includes: Extended tasting menu in private dining

exp-shepherds-river-table.name: The River Table
exp-shepherds-river-table.description: Fen Ditton sits on the Cam on the edge of Cambridge — and Ancient Shepherds' private River Table takes full advantage of it. A small group is welcomed into the restaurant after hours for a personal tasting menu from Mark Poynton, built around the finest seasonal produce of Cambridgeshire and the East Anglian coast: line-caught fish, artisan dairy from Fen Farm, hand-picked crab and the heritage vegetables of local growers. The meal is paired with a carefully chosen wine flight and delivered with the warmth and intimacy of a restaurant that is, at heart, a true neighbourhood table.
includes: Private use of the River Table
Personal tasting menu by Mark Poynton

northcote.quote: Lisa Goodwin-Allen is one of the finest chefs in Britain, and Northcote one of the most beautiful dining destinations in the north. This is British cooking at its most honest and most thrilling.
northcote.author: Giles Coren

northcote.quote: Northcote under Goodwin-Allen is a beacon of northern excellence — bold, seasonal and deeply rooted in the Ribble Valley landscape.
northcote.author: Grace Dent

three-fishes.quote: Haworth is back where he belongs, and the food is magnificent — English cooking at the very top of its game.
three-fishes.author: Gordon Sharpe

solo.quote: Tim Allen has created something extraordinary in Aughton. The Michelin star was inevitable — this is cooking of rare intelligence and beauty.
solo.author: Emma Whitelaw

the-kitchin.quote: The Kitchin remains Scotland's finest table — Tom Kitchin's 'From Nature to Plate' philosophy delivered with extraordinary skill and genuine emotion.
the-kitchin.author: Jay Rayner

scran-scallie.quote: The best gastropub in Scotland, and one of the most genuinely enjoyable places to eat in the country. The Kitchin touch is everywhere.
scran-scallie.author: Catriona Thomson

martin-wishart.quote: Twenty-five years and not a single year's doubt about the star. Martin Wishart has defined what it means to cook at this level in Scotland.
martin-wishart.author: Tom Peterkin

chez-bruce.quote: Chez Bruce is the restaurant Londoners vote for when asked to name their favourite — and rightly so. This is cooking from the heart, executed with the deepest craft.
chez-bruce.author: Grace Dent

la-trompette.quote: The jewel in Chiswick's culinary crown. La Trompette has prospered for twenty-five years because it follows a simple formula and executes it with flawless consistency.
la-trompette.author: Nick Lander

pied-a-terre.quote: London's longest-standing Michelin star restaurant remains every bit as thrilling as ever. Alberto Cavaliere is opening an exciting new chapter.
pied-a-terre.author: Dr Adrian York

galvin-la-chapelle.quote: One of London's most beautiful dining rooms — and the food is equal to the setting. The Galvins have created something genuinely magnificent.
galvin-la-chapelle.author: Tatler Restaurant of the Year

angel-hetton.quote: Michael Wignall is doing extraordinary things in the Yorkshire Dales. The Angel at Hetton is the most technically impressive gastropub in Britain.
angel-hetton.author: NRA Inspector

star-inn-harome.quote: Andrew Pern has been doing this for thirty years and has never been better. The Star at Harome is a national treasure and entirely irreplaceable.
star-inn-harome.author: Catherine Scott

pipe-glass.quote: East Yorkshire's finest dining destination — warm, generous, technically superb and deeply rooted in the landscape that surrounds it.
pipe-glass.author: GFG Inspector

restaurant-21.quote: Terry Laybourne built the dining culture of this city almost single-handedly. 21 remains its finest expression — warm, skilled and genuinely great.
restaurant-21.author: Tom Pattison

art-school.quote: Taste of England Award Winner. Paul Askew has given Liverpool the world-class gastronomic destination it always deserved.
art-school.author: Visit England Awards

opheem.quote: Aktar Islam is transforming the perception of Indian cuisine in this country. Two Michelin stars. Birmingham's finest. A restaurant of genuine historic significance.
opheem.author: Lauren Taylor

opheem.quote: Opheem is the most exciting Indian restaurant in Britain. Aktar Islam cooks with the intensity of a chef who has something profound to say.
opheem.author: Jay Rayner

el-gato-negro.quote: The best tapas in the north — and one of the most joyful dining rooms in England. Simon Shaw has built something truly special in Manchester.
el-gato-negro.author: Peter Harden

mark-poynton-caistor.quote: Technically adept, ingredient-led cooking in a beautiful Norfolk setting. If there is a chocolate dessert on the menu, you are in for a treat.
mark-poynton-caistor.author: Michelin Inspector

ancient-shepherds.quote: Ancient Shepherds is the kind of restaurant that makes people want to live in Fen Ditton. Mark Poynton cooks with quiet brilliance and total honesty.
ancient-shepherds.author: Julia Hayward
